reference: GATE CS — Operating System / Memory Management

map.condition: Max file size with inode (Unix-style direct + indirect blocks)
Usable data per block in linked allocation
Number of disk blocks addressable by FAT with k-bit entries
Max file size with index allocation (single-level index block)
Total disk capacity from block count and block size

formulas.name: FAT: Maximum number of addressable disk blocks
formulas.whenToUse: When FAT uses k-bit entries, the total number of disk blocks it can address is 2^k.

terms.means: number of bits per FAT entry
maximum number of distinct block addresses

formulas.trap: Do not confuse the number of FAT entries with the size of the FAT table in bytes; FAT size in bytes = (total blocks) x (k/8).

formulas.name: FAT: Size of the FAT table
formulas.whenToUse: To find how many bytes the FAT occupies on disk given N total blocks and k bits per entry.
formulas.trap: If k = 8, each entry is 1 byte; if k = 16 or 32, entries are 2 or 4 bytes. Watch for alignment or rounding to full blocks.

formulas.name: FAT: Total disk capacity from block count and size
formulas.whenToUse: When you know the number of blocks N and block size B, compute total disk capacity.

terms.means: size of each disk block in bytes

formulas.trap: Remember block 0 (or other reserved blocks) may be used by the FAT/directory and is not available for file data.

formulas.name: FAT: Maximum file size (limited by addressable blocks)
formulas.whenToUse: When FAT entry width k and block size B are given, find the largest possible file.
formulas.trap: The actual disk may be smaller than 2^k * B; max file size is min(disk size, 2^k * B).

formulas.name: Linked Allocation: Usable data bytes per block
formulas.plain: Usable data per block = B - P
formulas.whenToUse: In linked allocation each block stores a pointer to the next block, so data capacity per block is reduced by the pointer size.

terms.means: pointer size in bytes (typically 4 bytes for 32-bit)

formulas.trap: A common mistake is to use full block size B as usable data, ignoring the P bytes reserved for the next-block pointer.

formulas.name: Linked Allocation: Number of blocks needed for a file
formulas.whenToUse: To find how many disk blocks are required to store a file of size F bytes using linked allocation.
formulas.trap: Use ceiling (not floor) division; partial blocks still consume a full block.

formulas.name: Linked Allocation: Storage overhead ratio
formulas.whenToUse: To express what fraction of disk space is wasted on pointers in linked allocation.
formulas.trap: Overhead decreases with larger block sizes; but larger blocks increase internal fragmentation.

formulas.name: Inode: Maximum file size (Unix-style with indirect blocks)
formulas.whenToUse: For a Unix inode with D direct pointers, and single/double/triple indirect blocks, each holding B/A pointers.

terms.means: number of direct block pointers in inode (e.g. 10 in classic Unix)
size of a block address/pointer in bytes
number of block pointers that fit in one indirect block

formulas.trap: Forget to multiply the total block count by B to convert to bytes; also remember to include D direct blocks.

formulas.name: Inode: Pointers per indirect block
formulas.whenToUse: To find how many block addresses fit in one indirect block, used for computing levels of indirection.
formulas.trap: If block size is given in KB and pointer size in bytes, convert units before dividing.

formulas.name: Single Indirect Block: Data blocks reachable
formulas.plain: Blocks via single indirect = B / A
formulas.whenToUse: Count how many data blocks are addressable through one level of indirection.
formulas.trap: The single indirect block itself uses one block pointer in the inode but is NOT counted as a data block.

formulas.name: Double Indirect Block: Data blocks reachable
formulas.whenToUse: Count data blocks addressable through two levels of indirection.
formulas.trap: Two intermediate index blocks are consumed (not counted as data blocks) before reaching actual data blocks.

formulas.name: Triple Indirect Block: Data blocks reachable
formulas.whenToUse: Count data blocks addressable through three levels of indirection.
formulas.trap: Three intermediate index blocks are consumed before reaching data; do not add them to the file size.

formulas.name: Index Allocation: Max file size (single index block)
formulas.whenToUse: When one full disk block is used as an index block containing pointers to data blocks.
formulas.trap: The index block itself is not a data block; do not count it toward file size.

formulas.name: Index Allocation: Max file size (m index blocks)
formulas.plain: Max file size = m * (B / A) * B
formulas.whenToUse: When m index blocks are used, each pointing to B/A data blocks.
formulas.trap: More index blocks increase max file size linearly, but disk must have enough free blocks to hold all m index blocks plus data blocks.

formulas.name: One-Level Directory: All files in a single directory
formulas.plain: Single directory => all files share one namespace (no subdirectories)
formulas.whenToUse: When the problem states a one-level (flat) directory structure — no path resolution needed, but no name reuse across users.

terms.means: set of all unique file names visible in the directory

formulas.trap: In a one-level directory, two users cannot have files with the same name, unlike two-level or hierarchical directories.

formulas.name: Two-Level Directory: Per-user master directory
formulas.plain: MFD -> UFD_1, UFD_2, ... (master file directory points to user file directories)
formulas.whenToUse: When the system has a master file directory (MFD) with one user file directory (UFD) per user.
formulas.trap: Users can share names across UFDs, but sharing files between users requires special mechanisms (links or copying).

formulas.name: Contiguous Allocation: Key properties
formulas.latex: \text{Access time: } O(1) \text{ (direct seek); suffers external fragmentation}
formulas.plain: Contiguous: O(1) direct access, suffers external fragmentation, max file size fixed at creation
formulas.whenToUse: When comparing allocation methods — choose contiguous for sequential/random access performance.

terms.means: constant-time access to any block given start address and block number

formulas.trap: Contiguous allocation suffers from external fragmentation and requires knowing the file size at creation time.

formulas.name: Allocation Methods: Access-time comparison
formulas.plain: Random access speed: Contiguous (best) > Indexed > Linked (worst for random)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked to compare or rank file allocation methods by access performance.

terms.means: direct block access, best random access
1-3 extra disk reads for indirect blocks
must traverse chain for random access — O(n) seeks

formulas.trap: Linked allocation is efficient for sequential access but terrible for random access; indexed is the middle ground.
